title: Verraco Doesn't Need Permission to Rewrite Club Music
subtitle: The Medellin producer's XL Recordings debut is Latin techno at its most precise, playful, and forward-thinking
author: Leelou Blanc
body:
[spotify:track/0i9cabTVUzUfIN9OSmGvx0]

JP Lopez has spent the better part of a decade building something that the European techno establishment didn't think was supposed to come from Medellin. As Verraco, he's constructed a sound that treats Colombian rhythm, UK bass culture, and Berlin-grade sound design as equal ingredients rather than a hierarchy -- and with his 2025 debut on XL Recordings, **Basic Maneuvers**, the argument is no longer theoretical. It's settled.

## From Journalism to the Booth

[youtube:tIqy6WTSglY]

Lopez studied journalism at university in Bogota before abandoning the profession to make the music he'd been writing about. "My mentors in journalism gave me the tools to better understand my identity," he told Mixmag, "which set me up to better conceptualize projects like Insurgentes and my work as Verraco." That intellectual rigor shows in every production decision. There are no accidents in Verraco tracks. The dembow rhythms that surface in his club sets aren't nostalgic gestures -- they're structural choices, load-bearing walls in architecture that also happens to make people move.

His debut album **Grial** (2020) established the vocabulary: IDM textures, analogue techno warmth, and a distinctly Colombian sense of rhythmic complexity. But it was the **Escandaloo** EP on Blawan and Pariah's Voam label in 2023 that changed the trajectory. The title track became the summer anthem nobody predicted -- a piece of club music so catchy, so texturally rich, and so rhythmically disorienting that RA and Crack Magazine both named it among the year's best releases.

## TraTraTrax and the Scene

As co-founder of TraTraTrax alongside Nyksan and DJ Lomalinda, Lopez helped build a label that Resident Advisor famously compared to "this decade's Hessle Audio." The comparison makes sense structurally -- both labels operate as scenes unto themselves, curating a sonic identity that's bigger than any single artist. TraTraTrax has released music from producers across Latin America, from Bogota to Ecuador, and commissioned remixes from Pearson Sound and Perko that bridge the gap between the South American underground and the European club circuit.

But Lopez has been careful to resist the "Latin club" label that journalists keep trying to pin on the work. "We're just focused on the next move," he says. The point isn't to represent a geography. The point is to make music that's undeniable regardless of where the passport says you're from.

## The Sound Design Philosophy

Verraco's production is built on a refusal of easy hierarchy. In most techno, there is a clear structure: kick drum at the foundation, bass line providing movement, synths and percussion layered above. Verraco collapses this hierarchy. The dembow patterns that surface in his tracks do not sit below the techno elements or above them. They are woven into the same frequency space, competing and cooperating simultaneously.

The result is music that is difficult to analyze by decomposing it into layers, because the layers are not cleanly separable. This is deliberate. Lopez has talked about wanting to make music where the rhythm is the texture and the texture is the rhythm, where you cannot isolate one element without changing the meaning of the others.

His mixing decisions reinforce this. The low end in Verraco tracks is full but not boomy. The sub frequencies are controlled with the discipline of someone trained in the European techno tradition, but they are not the dominant element. The mid frequencies, where the dembow patterns live, carry as much weight as anything below them. This is not a conventional balance for club music, and it requires a sophisticated system and a sophisticated listener to fully register.

## The Escandaloo EP as Blueprint

Before Basic Maneuvers on XL, the Escandaloo EP on Voam established the template. The title track became the summer anthem nobody predicted, a piece of club music so catchy and so rhythmically disorienting that Resident Advisor and Crack Magazine both named it among 2023's best releases. That combination, catchy and disorienting at the same time, is what separates Verraco from producers who are merely interesting.

Most club music resolves the tension between accessibility and complexity by choosing one. Escandaloo chose both. The hook is real and immediate. The rhythmic structure underneath it will reward six months of listening with elements you did not notice on first pass. This is the highest bar in club music and Verraco clears it consistently.

Blawan and Pariah built Voam as a platform for club music that refuses genre safety, and the Verraco EP was the clearest argument for what the label stands for. That Lopez's next significant release would land at XL, a label with the cultural weight to bring his work to a genuinely broad audience, was the logical next step. It was not a sell-out. It was an arrival.

## Geography as Creative Resource

Lopez has been explicit about Medellin as a creative resource rather than a limitation. The city's musical culture, which runs from cumbia through champeta to reggaeton and back, provides a rhythmic vocabulary that Berlin and London simply do not have. He has absorbed that vocabulary and brought it into dialogue with the production traditions of the European techno circuit without flattening either.

The result is music that sounds like nowhere else, which is the correct outcome when you take two genuinely distinct musical traditions seriously and refuse to privilege one over the other. His journalism training, the analytical instinct to understand what you are writing about before you write it, shows here. He did not arrive at this synthesis accidentally. He understood what he was combining and why.

## Basic Maneuvers

The XL Recordings EP consolidates everything. Three tracks that move between hypnotic techno, bass-heavy groove, and the kind of rhythmic invention that makes you forget what time signature you're supposed to be counting. Lopez's production has never sounded more confident -- the tracks are sparse where they need to be sparse, dense where density serves the dance floor, and consistently surprising without ever losing the thread.

With Dekmantel, Berghain, and Berlin Atonal already on his CV, and an Escandaloo party series in Bogota that's sold out every edition, Verraco has built a world. Basic Maneuvers is just the latest invitation to enter it.

**Find Verraco on Instagram: [@verraco__](https://instagram.com/verraco__)**
